reviews.summary: The converted church setting is charming. The beer range is broad and consistent. Some find the food more pub-standard than restaurant-quality.

pois.name: Palate Restaurant
pois.short: Hamilton's only two-hat restaurant — modern NZ cuisine with exceptional local produce.
pois.commentary: Palate holds the distinction of being Hamilton's highest-rated restaurant in the Cuisine Good Food Guide, with two hats. The tasting menus showcase Waikato and NZ produce with technical precision — expect intricate dishes that reward close attention. The wine list is thoughtfully curated, and the service is knowledgeable without being stuffy. If you're eating one meal in Hamilton, this should be it.

reviews.summary: Hamilton's best restaurant, full stop. The tasting menus are creative and well-executed. Some find the fine-dining formality at odds with Hamilton's casual character, but the food quality is undeniable.

pois.name: Hayes Common
pois.short: Community-focused Hamilton eatery — creative food, craft beer and a neighbourhood gathering spot.
pois.commentary: Hayes Common was built on the idea that every community needs a place with creative food, good beer and a welcoming atmosphere. Owners Lisa and Brent Quarrie (both trained chefs) serve a menu that changes with the seasons, using local Waikato producers. The craft beer selection is well-chosen, and the space — part café, part restaurant, part bar — adapts to the time of day. A Hamilton favourite.

reviews.summary: The community-focused concept resonates. The food is creative and well-priced. Some find the multi-purpose format a bit confusing — café by day, restaurant by night.

pois.name: Hamilton Farmers' Market
pois.short: Sunday morning riverside market — Waikato's best produce, baking and artisan food on the river bank.
pois.commentary: The Hamilton Farmers' Market sets up along the Waikato River bank every Sunday morning, showcasing the region's rich agricultural heritage. Expect dairy products (the Waikato is NZ's dairy heartland), seasonal berries, artisan cheeses, fresh bread and locally produced honey. The riverside setting is pleasant, and there's good coffee and street food. Smaller than Auckland or Wellington's markets, but the produce quality is excellent.

reviews.summary: Good quality local produce in a pleasant riverside setting. The dairy products reflect the Waikato's heritage. Some find the range limited compared to larger city markets.

pois.name: Raglan Food Scene
pois.short: Surf town with a food scene that punches above its weight — organic cafés, bakeries and the famous Raglan Roast.
pois.commentary: Raglan is a surf town with a food culture that belies its small size. The Shack (wood-fired pizza), Raglan Roast (locally roasted coffee), Solscape's organic café and the Raglan Wholefoods co-op all reflect the town's environmentally conscious character. The Sunday farmers' market is small but quality-driven. After eating, walk along the harbour or drive to Manu Bay for some of the Southern Hemisphere's best left-hand breaks.

reviews.summary: The organic, surf-town food culture is charming. The coffee scene is excellent. Some restaurants have limited winter hours.

pois.name: Chim Choo Ree
pois.short: Hamilton fine dining in the former Waikato Brewery — sleek industrial space with creative seasonal menus.
pois.commentary: Chim Choo Ree occupies the charming historic former Waikato Brewery building, with sleek wooden floors, white tiles and quirky artwork creating a distinctive atmosphere. The seasonal menus showcase Waikato produce with creativity and precision — this is serious cooking in an unexpected Hamilton setting. The cocktail list is creative, and the wine list champions small NZ producers.

reviews.summary: A delightful surprise in Hamilton. The heritage building adds character. Some find the innovative menu items challenging, but most appreciate the ambition.

pois.name: Over the Moon Dairy
pois.short: Putāruru artisan cheese maker — handcrafted NZ cheeses from Waikato milk with tastings.
pois.commentary: Over the Moon makes exceptional artisan cheeses using milk from Waikato farms. The range includes soft, semi-hard and hard cheeses, many with creative flavour additions — their Galactic Gold (a washed-rind cheese) and Cranberry Brie are standouts. The small shop in Putāruru offers tastings and you can buy directly. It's a great Waikato detour between Auckland and Rotorua, and the cheeses make excellent food gifts.

reviews.summary: Excellent artisan cheese from local milk. The tastings are generous. The Putāruru location is off the main highway but worth the detour.

pois.name: Scotts Brewing Co
pois.short: Cambridge craft brewery — small-batch beers brewed in the Waikato's charming horse-town.
pois.commentary: Scotts Brewing Co operates from Cambridge, the attractive Waikato town known for thoroughbred horses and tree-lined streets. The taproom pours a range of well-crafted beers — the NZ Pilsner and APA are both excellent — in a relaxed setting. Cambridge is a nice detour between Hamilton and Hobbiton, and Scotts provides the perfect refreshment stop. The outdoor courtyard catches the afternoon sun.

reviews.summary: Good small-batch beers in a pleasant setting. Cambridge is a nice town to explore. Limited hours — check before visiting.

pois.name: Maungatautari Ecological Island
pois.short: NZ's largest predator-fenced mainland island — 3,400 hectares of bush with takahē, kiwi, and tuatara.
pois.commentary: Maungatautari is enclosed by 47 km of predator-proof fence, making it the largest mainland island in NZ. Takahē, kiwi, tuatara, hihi (stitchbird), and kōkako have been released into the pest-free bush. Two visitor entry points offer guided and self-guided walks.

reviews.summary: Incredible conservation achievement. The Tautari Wetland walk is excellent. Guided tours recommended for best wildlife viewing.

pois.name: Miranda Shorebird Centre / Pūkorokoro
pois.short: Internationally significant shorebird site — bar-tailed godwits arrive after 11,000 km non-stop from Alaska.
pois.commentary: The Miranda/Pūkorokoro Shorebird Centre on the Firth of Thames is one of the most important shorebird sites in NZ. Bar-tailed godwits (kuaka) arrive each September after an 11,000 km non-stop flight from Alaska. The centre provides binoculars, hides, and expert guidance.

reviews.summary: World-class birding. The shorebird centre is excellent — knowledgeable staff. Low tide is essential. Spring migration peak.

pois.name: Waitomo Glowworm Caves
pois.short: NZ's most famous glowworm display — boat ride through caverns lit by millions of bioluminescent larvae.
pois.commentary: The Waitomo Glowworm Caves are one of NZ's most iconic attractions. A guided boat ride through the caves reveals a galaxy of Arachnocampa luminosa glowworms on the cave ceiling. The experience is magical and suitable for all ages. Multiple cave experiences available from gentle to adventure.

reviews.summary: Must-do NZ experience. The glowworm boat ride is magical. Book ahead in peak season. Various experience levels available.

pois.name: Ruakuri Cave — Cave Ecology Experience
pois.short: Deep cave system with glowworms, cave weta, and ancient stalactites — more extensive than the main Waitomo cave.
pois.commentary: Ruakuri Cave offers a deeper cave experience than the main Waitomo cave, with extensive formations and impressive glowworm displays. Cave weta (large insects) are often visible. The spiral entrance is architecturally stunning. The cave ecology interpretation is excellent.

reviews.summary: Better than the main cave for many visitors. More extensive and less crowded. The formations are spectacular.

pois.name: Hamilton Lake / Rotoroa — Urban Waterbirds
pois.short: Urban lake with black swan, shag, dabchick, and seasonal wading birds — easy lakeside walk.
pois.commentary: Hamilton Lake (Lake Rotoroa) in central Hamilton is a pleasant urban birding site. Black swan, little shag, NZ dabchick, grey teal, and paradise shelduck are resident. The lakeside loop walk is easy and popular. Native plantings around the margins attract bush birds.

reviews.summary: Easy urban birding. Good variety for a city lake. Pleasant walk at any time of day.

pois.name: Karangahake Gorge — Bush Bird Walk
pois.short: Gold mining heritage walk through regenerating bush with excellent tui, kereru, and North Island robin.
pois.commentary: The Karangahake Gorge walkway follows the Ohinemuri River through regenerating bush and gold mining ruins. The bush holds excellent populations of tui, kereru, fantail, and North Island robin. The Windows Walk (mining tunnel) and suspension bridges add adventure.

reviews.summary: Great walk combining history and nature. The robins are very tame. The Windows Walk is atmospheric.

pois.name: Hakarimata Scenic Reserve — Kaka & Kōkako
pois.short: Regenerating bush near Huntly where kaka and kokako are returning thanks to community pest control.
pois.commentary: The Hakarimata Scenic Reserve behind Ngaruawahia and Huntly has seen remarkable bird recovery thanks to community trapping. Kaka have returned, kokako are occasionally heard, and tui and bellbird populations have surged. The track to the summit is a popular fitness walk.

reviews.summary: Inspiring recovery story. The summit walk is steep but rewarding. Dawn birding is excellent.

pois.name: Whale Bay — Marine Ecology
pois.short: Rich rock pool ecology on the Raglan coast — sea anemones, starfish, and diverse intertidal life.
pois.commentary: Whale Bay near Raglan has extensive rock platforms exposed at low tide, revealing diverse intertidal ecology. Sea anemones, cushion stars, chitons, and various crab species inhabit the pools. The bay is also popular for surfing and has a beautiful bush-lined beach.

reviews.summary: Great rock pooling at low tide. Beautiful setting. Combine with Raglan surfing and cafe culture.

pois.name: Waingaro Hot Springs — Giant Eels
pois.short: Giant longfin eels in the streams around the hot springs — some estimated at 80+ years old.
pois.commentary: The streams near Waingaro Hot Springs hold giant longfin eels, some estimated to be over 80 years old. Feeding sessions allow close viewing of these ancient endemic fish. The hot springs themselves are a family-friendly attraction.

reviews.summary: Fascinating eel viewing. The hot springs are fun for families. The eels are impressively large.

pois.name: Pirongia Forest Park — Bush Bird Recovery
pois.short: Intensive predator control bringing bush birds back to the Waikato's highest peak — kokako, kiwi, and kaka.
pois.commentary: Pirongia Forest Park on the Waikato's highest peak is a focus for intensive predator control. Kokako have been successfully reintroduced, kiwi numbers are increasing, and kaka are returning. The summit track is challenging but the forest is magnificent.

reviews.summary: Excellent bush birding on a rewarding tramp. The kokako reintroduction is a success story. Summit track is challenging.

pois.name: Whangamarino Wetland — Ramsar Site
pois.short: Internationally significant Ramsar wetland — bittern, fernbird, and marsh crake in vast peat bogs.
pois.commentary: Whangamarino Wetland near Meremere is a Ramsar site of international importance, protecting 7,290 hectares of peat bog, swamp, and open water. Australasian bittern, fernbird, spotless crake, and NZ dabchick inhabit the wetland. Access is limited but rewarding for dedicated birders.

reviews.summary: Significant wetland — one of NZ's largest. Difficult access but important for serious birders. Bittern are present.

pois.name: Kāwhia Harbour — Estuarine Birds
pois.short: Sheltered harbour with bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, and white-faced heron.
pois.commentary: Kāwhia Harbour is a large sheltered harbour on the Waikato west coast that supports good populations of wading birds. Bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, white-faced heron, and variable oystercatcher are regular. The harbour is peaceful and uncrowded, offering a quiet birding experience.

reviews.summary: Peaceful harbour birding. Off the tourist trail. Good wader variety at low tide.

pois.name: Waikato Museum Te Whare Taonga o Waikato
pois.short: Hamilton's regional museum featuring the magnificent Te Winika waka taua (war canoe) and rich Waikato stories.
pois.commentary: Waikato Museum Te Whare Taonga o Waikato is Hamilton's principal cultural institution, located on the banks of the Waikato River in the central city. The museum's greatest treasure is Te Winika, a magnificent 200-year-old waka taua (war canoe) that is one of the finest examples of its type in New Zealand. The intricately carved canoe is displayed in a purpose-built gallery with full cultural context. The museum presents a dynamic programme covering Waikato regional history, Tainui Māori heritage, art exhibitions, and interactive science displays. The Waikato River story — from its volcanic origins at Mount Ruapehu through the rich plains to the sea — threads through many exhibitions. Admission to the permanent collection is free. The museum also hosts the ArtsPost gallery for contemporary art in the adjoining heritage building. The riverside location is attractive, with walking paths along the river. Hamilton's dining scene has improved dramatically in recent years, with excellent options within walking distance.

reviews.summary: The Te Winika waka is genuinely awe-inspiring — a masterpiece of Māori carving. The museum does a great job of telling the Waikato story. Free entry to the permanent collection is generous.

pois.name: Waikato Regional Theatre (Momentum)
pois.short: Hamilton's newest performing arts venue bringing world-class shows and concerts to the Waikato region.
pois.commentary: The Waikato Regional Theatre (also known as Momentum) represents a major investment in Hamilton's cultural infrastructure, providing the city and wider Waikato region with a modern, purpose-built performing arts venue. The theatre hosts touring musicals, concerts, ballet, opera, comedy, and major events, filling a long-standing gap in the region's cultural facilities. The main auditorium offers excellent acoustics and sightlines, while smaller spaces cater to intimate performances and community events. The venue's riverside location in central Hamilton connects it to the city's evolving cultural precinct alongside the Waikato Museum and ArtsPost gallery. The theatre brings productions that previously bypassed Hamilton on the touring circuit, significantly enriching the region's cultural offerings. Check the programme for current shows — the venue attracts major New Zealand and international touring productions. The surrounding area offers good dining options for pre and post-show meals, and the riverside walkway makes for a pleasant approach.

reviews.summary: Great to finally have a proper theatre in Hamilton — the venue is modern and comfortable with good sound. The programming is varied and getting better. A real asset for the Waikato region.

pois.name: Tirau Corrugated Iron Sculptures
pois.short: Quirky Waikato town famous for its giant corrugated iron animal buildings, a classic Kiwi road trip stop.
pois.commentary: Tirau is a small South Waikato town that has transformed itself into one of New Zealand's quirkiest roadside attractions through a collection of giant corrugated iron sculptures and animal-shaped buildings. The most famous is the massive corrugated iron dog that houses the information centre, alongside a corrugated iron sheep and various other metal creatures dotted through the town. Local artist and farmer Jeff Thomson pioneered the corrugated iron art movement, and Tirau embraced his vision wholeheartedly. The town on State Highway 1 between Hamilton and Rotorua/Taupō has reinvented itself as 'The Corrugated Capital,' with the sculptures drawing thousands of visitors who stop for photos and end up browsing the antique shops, cafés, and quirky retail stores that now line the main street. Tirau demonstrates how creative thinking can revitalise a small rural town. Stop for photos and a coffee — the main street shops are worth a browse, especially the antique and collectibles stores. A fun, free, and authentically Kiwi experience.

reviews.summary: Such a fun little town — the giant corrugated iron animals are brilliantly quirky. The antique shops are worth browsing and the cafés are decent. A perfect road trip pitstop between Hamilton and Rotorua.

pois.name: Raglan Surf Beaches
pois.short: World-famous left-hand point breaks at Manu Bay and Whale Bay, the heart of NZ's surf culture.
pois.commentary: Raglan's surf beaches are legendary — the town's west coast location produces some of the longest and most consistent left-hand point breaks in the world. Manu Bay (Indicators) is the most famous break, offering a long, perfectly shaped left-hander that was featured in the classic 1966 surf film 'The Endless Summer.' Whale Bay, accessible via a bush track, provides another excellent left in a beautiful, secluded setting. Ngarunui Beach (the main ocean beach) offers more forgiving conditions suitable for learners, with surf schools operating in summer. The town of Raglan itself is one of New Zealand's most characterful communities — a creative, environmentally conscious surf town with excellent cafés, craft breweries, and a thriving arts scene. The harbour is beautiful and the sunsets are legendary. Raglan is about 45 minutes west of Hamilton, making it accessible for day trips, though most visitors want to stay longer. The surf community is welcoming but respectful of localism at the premium breaks. Surf lessons at Ngarunui are recommended for beginners.

reviews.summary: A world-class surf destination with incredible left-handers and an amazing town vibe. Even if you don't surf, Raglan's cafés, creativity, and sunsets are worth the trip. One of NZ's most special towns.

pois.name: Waitomo Black Abyss
pois.short: Extreme caving adventure in Waitomo with a 35m abseil, flying fox, and black water rafting through glow-worm caves.
pois.commentary: The Black Abyss is the most extreme caving experience offered at Waitomo, combining a 35-metre underground abseil into the Ruakuri Cave system, a subterranean flying fox over a chasm, and black water tubing through underground rivers illuminated by thousands of glow-worms. The five-hour adventure begins with training and a dramatic descent into the darkness, followed by a series of underground challenges including rock scrambling, swimming, and floating through cathedral-sized chambers. The glow-worm displays, viewed while floating silently on the underground river, are ethereal and unforgettable. No prior caving experience is required but reasonable fitness is needed. Wetsuits, helmets, and all equipment are provided. The experience operates year-round with departures twice daily. Minimum age is 16 years. Hot showers and soup are provided afterwards. Book well ahead during peak season as numbers are strictly limited per trip.

reviews.summary: An absolutely unforgettable adventure that pushes boundaries in the most spectacular underground environment. The abseil into darkness is thrilling and the glow-worm displays are magical. Expect to get cold and wet but love every minute.

pois.name: Hamilton Zoo
pois.short: Regional zoo with free-flight aviary, native wildlife encounters, and exotic species set in native bush.
pois.commentary: Hamilton Zoo is the largest zoo in the North Island by area, set across 25 hectares of landscaped grounds and native bush in the western hills of Hamilton. The zoo houses over 500 animals from more than 70 species, with a strong focus on conservation and native wildlife. The free-flight aviary is one of the Southern Hemisphere's largest, featuring native forest birds including kaka, kereru, and tui in a walk-through bush environment. Other highlights include the cheetah enclosure, Sumatran tiger exhibit, and a reptile house featuring tuatara. The zoo's location within the Waiwhakareke Natural Heritage Park, a significant ecological restoration project, adds a conservation dimension. Behind-the-scenes experiences and keeper talks run throughout the day. The zoo is well-designed for families with good pathways, a playground, and picnic areas. Located 10 minutes from central Hamilton on Brymer Road.

reviews.summary: A well-maintained regional zoo with impressive native bird collections. The free-flight aviary is a highlight. Good family outing with enough variety to fill a morning or afternoon.

pois.name: Hobbiton Movie Set
pois.short: The real Hobbiton movie set from The Lord of the Rings and The Hobbit films, preserved on a Matamata farm.
pois.commentary: Hobbiton Movie Set, nestled in the rolling green farmland near Matamata, is the actual filming location used for the Shire sequences in Peter Jackson's The Lord of the Rings and The Hobbit trilogies. The set was rebuilt in permanent materials for The Hobbit films and has been preserved as a visitor attraction since 2012. Guided tours take visitors through 44 hobbit holes, the Party Tree, the Mill, and the double-arched bridge, all set against the backdrop of the Kaimai Ranges. The tour concludes at The Green Dragon Inn, where guests enjoy a complimentary drink in a fully realised Hobbiton pub. The attention to detail is extraordinary, from the tiny garden tools to the washing on the line. Tours depart from the Shire's Rest cafe and travel by bus to the set. Booking is essential and tours often sell out days in advance during peak season. Photography is encouraged throughout.

reviews.summary: A must-visit for Tolkien fans and an impressive experience even for non-fans. The level of detail is remarkable and the setting genuinely magical. The Green Dragon drink is a perfect finishing touch.

pois.name: Te Aroha Mineral Spas
pois.short: Historic thermal spa town with natural mineral hot pools and the only hot soda water geyser in the Southern Hemisphere.
pois.commentary: Te Aroha is a charming small town at the foot of Mount Te Aroha in the eastern Waikato, renowned for its natural hot soda water springs, the only ones in New Zealand. The Te Aroha Mineral Spas complex offers private and public soaking pools filled with naturally heated mineral water, set in the historic Domain that dates back to the 1880s when Te Aroha was a fashionable spa resort. The Mokena Geyser, the only known hot soda water geyser in the Southern Hemisphere, erupts periodically in the Domain. The town retains Victorian-era character with heritage buildings, a museum in the former cadets' hall, and the beautifully maintained Domain gardens. The Hauraki Rail Trail passes through town, making Te Aroha a natural stop for cyclists. A walk to the summit of Mount Te Aroha offers panoramic views across the Hauraki Plains and Coromandel Peninsula. The mineral spas are open daily and booking is recommended for private pools.

reviews.summary: A lovely heritage spa town with unique mineral waters. The hot pools are relaxing and the Domain gardens are beautiful. The Mokena Geyser is a fascinating natural curiosity.

pois.name: Ngaruawahia River Regatta
pois.short: Annual Maori waka racing regatta on the Waikato River at Ngaruawahia, the seat of the Maori King movement.
pois.commentary: The Ngaruawahia River Regatta is one of New Zealand's oldest and most significant Maori cultural events, held annually on the Waikato River at Ngaruawahia, the seat of the Kiingitanga (Maori King movement). First held in 1896, the regatta features waka (canoe) races on the river, with teams paddling traditional and modern waka in a display of skill, strength, and cultural pride. The event also includes kapahaka (performing arts) competitions, traditional games, kai stalls, and cultural displays on the riverbank. The regatta takes place at The Point, the confluence of the Waikato and Waipa Rivers, a site of deep significance in Tainui history. Turangawaewae Marae, the principal marae of the Kiingitanga, provides the cultural backdrop. The regatta is open to the public and offers a genuine opportunity to experience Maori culture in its most heartfelt expression. Check dates annually, typically held in March.

reviews.summary: An authentic and exhilarating Maori cultural event with waka racing as the centrepiece. The atmosphere is festive and welcoming. A rare opportunity to experience living Maori culture at a significant historic site.

pois.name: Raglan Food & Coffee Scene
pois.short: Eclectic surf town food scene with organic cafes, artisan bakeries, and a thriving farmers market.
pois.commentary: Raglan is a small Waikato surf town with a disproportionately excellent food and coffee scene, driven by a creative community that values organic, local, and sustainable produce. The Raglan Wholefoods store is a community hub selling organic and locally sourced products. Numerous cafes along Bow Street and the main drag serve excellent coffee, vegetarian and vegan dishes, and casual dining that reflects the town's laid-back surf culture. The Raglan Farmers Market on Saturday mornings brings together local growers and artisan food producers. Notable stops include Raglan Roast for coffee, The Shack for burgers, and various food trucks in the wharf area. The town's commitment to zero waste and sustainable practices is evident throughout the food scene. Combined with the world-famous surf breaks at Manu Bay and Whale Bay, the harbour-side setting, and the creative community, Raglan offers a distinctly New Zealand coastal experience just two hours from Auckland.

reviews.summary: A surprisingly vibrant food scene for a small surf town. The commitment to organic and local produce is genuine. Great cafes, a lovely market, and the surf town atmosphere adds character.

pois.name: Hamilton Gardens
pois.short: Internationally acclaimed themed garden collection on the Waikato River, featuring gardens from around the world.
pois.commentary: Hamilton Gardens is one of New Zealand's most visited attractions and an internationally recognised garden of global significance. Rather than a traditional botanical garden, it presents a collection of themed garden rooms that tell the story of gardens through civilisation. The enclosed Paradise Gardens include an Italian Renaissance garden, Japanese garden of contemplation, Chinese Scholar's garden, Indian Char Bagh garden, Maori Te Parapara garden, and English Flower garden, each meticulously designed and maintained. The Productive Gardens showcase sustainable food growing including a medieval herb garden and an American modernist kitchen garden. Fantasy Gardens explore imaginative themes. Set alongside the Waikato River, the gardens offer walking tracks through native bush and riverside landscapes. Entry is free, making it accessible to all. Guided tours provide deeper insights into the design philosophy. Located on the southern edge of Hamilton, with ample parking and a cafe. The gardens have been compared favourably with some of Europe's finest.

reviews.summary: One of New Zealand's finest attractions, with themed gardens that transport you around the world. The attention to detail is extraordinary and free entry is remarkable. Allow more time than you expect.

pois.name: Waikato Museum Te Whare Taonga
pois.short: Hamilton's cultural heart — Māori taonga, art exhibitions, and the story of the Waikato River.
pois.commentary: Waikato Museum sits on the banks of the Waikato River in central Hamilton, housing significant Tainui taonga alongside art galleries and science exhibitions. The centrepiece is Te Winika, a 200-year-old waka taua (war canoe) displayed in a purpose-built gallery. Rotating exhibitions cover everything from contemporary Māori art to natural history. The museum's relationship with local iwi Waikato-Tainui gives the cultural displays an authenticity and depth that generic museums lack. Free entry and a good café make it an easy stop.

reviews.summary: Visitors praise the Tainui cultural displays and the riverside setting. Te Winika waka is consistently rated as the highlight. Free entry and central location make it very accessible.

pois.name: Raglan / Whāingaroa — Manu Bay
pois.short: Home of NZ's most famous left-hand break — a legendary surf town with a creative, laid-back vibe.
pois.commentary: Raglan is New Zealand's surf town. Manu Bay produces one of the longest and most consistent left-hand point breaks in the world — a wave that peels for up to 300 metres along the rocky shoreline. Even if you don't surf, Raglan's creative, alternative-lifestyle energy is infectious: excellent cafés, a thriving arts scene, and a stunning harbour setting. Whale Bay and Indicators offer more surf breaks within walking distance. The town itself is small enough to explore on foot, and the Raglan Wharf is perfect for a sunset walk.

reviews.summary: Surfers rate Manu Bay as a bucket-list wave. Non-surfers enjoy the town's creative energy and café scene. The laid-back atmosphere is praised as authentic, not manufactured.

pois.name: Ōtorohanga Kiwi House
pois.short: One of NZ's best kiwi viewing experiences — see all five species plus tuatara and native birds.
pois.commentary: Ōtorohanga Kiwi House is one of the best places in New Zealand to see kiwi — the nocturnal house replicates a natural forest floor at night, and the kiwi are active and visible. What sets this facility apart is the chance to see multiple kiwi species in one visit, including the rare rowi and Haast tokoeka. The native bird aviary includes kea, kākā, morepork, and tūī, while the reptile house has tuatara and native geckos. It's a genuine conservation facility, not a theme park.

reviews.summary: Families and wildlife enthusiasts praise the kiwi viewing experience as among the best in the country. The conservation focus is appreciated. A perfect stop en route to Waitomo Caves.

pois.name: Bridal Veil Falls / Waireinga
pois.short: A stunning 55m free-falling waterfall in a bush-clad gorge near Raglan — short walk to the base.
pois.commentary: Bridal Veil Falls / Waireinga is one of the Waikato's most spectacular natural features — a 55-metre free-fall waterfall dropping into a deep plunge pool in a bush-clad gorge. The walk from the car park to the upper lookout takes just 10 minutes, with the steeper track continuing down to the base for the full impact of the falls. The bush is lush and the gorge amplifies the sound of the water. Combine with a visit to nearby Raglan for a perfect day trip from Hamilton. The falls are at their most impressive after heavy rain.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the falls as stunning and easily accessible. The short walk to the base is praised. The Raglan combination makes it a perfect half-day outing.

pois.name: Forgotten World Adventures Rail Cart
pois.short: Self-drive rail carts through 20km of tunnels and bridges on an abandoned railway line — utterly unique NZ experience.
pois.commentary: Forgotten World Adventures offers one of New Zealand's most unique experiences: self-drive rail carts along the abandoned Stratford-Ōkahukura railway line through the remote Tangarakau Gorge. The journey passes through 20 tunnels (the longest is 1.5km — bring a torch), over bridges, and through some of the most isolated countryside in the North Island. The longest tour runs 42km and takes a full day. It's part heritage railway experience, part backcountry adventure, and entirely unlike anything else in the country. Book well ahead — it's popular.

reviews.summary: Visitors rate this as one of NZ's most unique activities. The tunnel experience is described as thrilling. Booking well in advance is essential — slots fill fast.

pois.name: Kawhia Hot Water Beach
pois.short: A secret hot water beach that most tourists don't know about — dig your own hot pool on the sand.
pois.commentary: Most visitors know about Hot Water Beach on the Coromandel, but few know about Kawhia's version — and locals like it that way. On Ocean Beach at low tide, hot spring water seeps through the black sand, and you can dig your own personal hot pool where geothermal heat meets the Tasman Sea. It's less developed, less crowded, and feels genuinely secret. The town of Kawhia itself is a quiet harbour community with deep Tainui connections — the Tainui waka is said to be buried nearby. Check tide times before you go — it only works at low tide.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the uncrowded, authentic feel compared to Coromandel's Hot Water Beach. The remote location adds to the appeal. Tide timing is essential — plan carefully.

pois.name: Zealong Tea Estate
pois.short: The only commercial tea estate in NZ — tours, tastings, and elegant high tea surrounded by camellia plants.
pois.commentary: Zealong is New Zealand's only commercial tea estate, growing organic camellia sinensis on the outskirts of Hamilton. The microclimate of the Waikato — warm, humid, and sheltered — produces teas that have won international awards. The estate offers guided tours through the plantations, tea tastings that educate your palate on the differences between black, green, and oolong varieties, and an elegant high tea service with panoramic views. It's a surprisingly refined experience in rural Waikato, and the gift shop is perfect for taking NZ-grown tea home.

reviews.summary: Visitors are charmed by the unexpected elegance of a tea estate in the Waikato. The high tea experience is rated excellent. Tours are educational and the setting is beautiful.

pois.name: Ruakuri Cave
pois.short: The longest underground walking tour in NZ — 1.6 km of cathedral-like limestone cave.
pois.commentary: Many consider it better than the more famous Waitomo Glowworm Caves. The modern entrance spirals underground via a dramatic ‘drum passage’.

reviews.summary: Many visitors prefer Ruakuri to the main Waitomo cave.

pois.name: Tūrangawaewae Marae, Ngāruawāhia
pois.short: The principal marae of the Māori King movement.
pois.commentary: Seat of the Kīngitanga since 1858. The annual Ngāruawāhia Regatta (March) is the only time generally open to the public.

reviews.summary: A site of immense cultural and political significance. The Regatta is a wonderful community event.

pois.name: ArtsPost Galleries and Shop, Hamilton
pois.short: Hamilton's beloved arts hub — a 1936 Art Deco post office turned gallery, design store, and artist studio space.
pois.commentary: ArtsPost occupies Hamilton's former main post office (1936), a handsome Art Deco building in the heart of the CBD. Three gallery spaces run rolling exhibitions of Waikato-based and national artists across painting, photography, ceramics, and textile art. The ground floor shop is one of the best places in the Waikato to buy original NZ art and craft. Entry to all gallery spaces is free. ArtsPost works closely with the Waikato Museum two blocks away — the two make a natural pairing. The building itself is architecturally significant and worth examining from the street before entering.

reviews.summary: A gem in central Hamilton. The galleries punch above their weight and the craft shop is excellent for authentic NZ-made gifts. Free entry makes it an easy stop.

pois.name: Cambridge Arts and Heritage Trail
pois.short: Cambridge's tree-lined streets conceal a thriving arts and antiques scene — galleries, studios, and one of NZ's best book towns.
pois.commentary: Cambridge markets itself as 'the town of trees and champions' and while the racehorses get most of the press, the arts and heritage offer is genuinely strong. Lake Road and Victoria Street house over a dozen galleries and artisan studios, several antique dealers, and the well-stocked Cambridge Book Exchange. St Andrew's Anglican Church (1881) is considered one of the finest timber Gothic churches in New Zealand. The Saturday morning market at the town hall is a fixture. Cambridge also holds an Arts in the Park festival every November. The town is 25 minutes from Hamilton and makes an ideal half-day addition to any Waikato itinerary.

reviews.summary: One of New Zealand's most pleasant small towns for a leisurely arts and heritage wander. The combination of churches, galleries, antiques, and cafes is well-executed. A relaxed and unhurried experience.

pois.name: Raglan Arts and Creative Community
pois.short: Raglan's bohemian creative scene — pottery studios, surf art, galleries, and the vibrant Raglan Artisan Market.
pois.commentary: Raglan has attracted artists, makers, and creative people for decades, drawn by the surf, the light, and the relaxed pace. The result is a creative scene that punches well above the town's 3,000-person population. Okurei Point Pottery, the Volcano Café precinct, and several independent galleries line Bow Street. The Raglan Artisan Market runs on the last Sunday of each month at the wharf, showcasing pottery, textiles, jewellery, and food. Local artists include nationally recognised painters and printmakers with studios open by appointment. The surf art thread running through Raglan's creative output is distinctive and worth seeking out.

reviews.summary: Raglan's creative scene is a genuine surprise. The combination of surf culture and serious art-making gives it a unique character. The Artisan Market is excellent and the pottery community is particularly strong.

pois.name: Tirau Corrugated Iron Arts Town
pois.short: The North Island's most eccentric roadside arts town — giant corrugated iron animals, antique shops, and rural NZ at its most exuberant.
pois.commentary: Tirau has reinvented itself through a commitment to spectacularly large corrugated iron sculptures. The giant sheep (i-SITE visitor centre), giant dog (crafts and gifts), giant ram, and giant rooster anchor the main street, which extends into antique dealers, craft galleries, and cafes catering to the State Highway 1 traffic. The architectural project started in 1994 and has become a genuine piece of NZ folk art vernacular. The antique shops carry good country-clearance stock. An hour is right — enjoy it for what it is and move on with a smile. Tirau sits at the junction of SH1 and SH27, making it almost unavoidable on a Waikato drive.

reviews.summary: A guaranteed mood-lifter on any road trip. The giant iron animals are impressive at close range. The antique shops are well-stocked. A perfect short stop on the way south.

pois.name: Hamilton Street Art Festival and River Quarter Murals
pois.short: Hamilton's revitalised River Quarter is home to an outstanding collection of large-scale street art murals — one of NZ's best urban art walks.
pois.commentary: Hamilton's annual Street Art Festival has produced an extraordinary collection of large-scale murals across the city centre and River Quarter, with walls by internationally recognised artists alongside emerging NZ talent. The permanent collection is substantial enough to merit a dedicated self-guided walk (maps available from Hamilton i-SITE). The River Quarter itself — a continuous pedestrian zone from the Waikato Museum to Fisherman's Wharf — activates the Waikato riverfront through public art, cafes, and seasonal events including the Night Noodle Markets and Balloons Over Waikato festival. The Frankton Rail Trail connects the precinct to the outer suburbs for cyclists.

reviews.summary: Hamilton's street art collection is world-class and consistently surprises visitors. The River Quarter is a lovely setting. The self-guided mural walk is excellent and completely free.

pois.name: Hobbiton Movie Set — Evening Banquet Experience
pois.short: The Hobbiton Movie Set evening banquet tour — the Shire lit by lanterns, dinner in the Green Dragon Inn.
pois.commentary: While the daytime tour of Hobbiton at Matamata is widely known, the Evening Banquet Experience is more immersive. Running October through April, the tour takes small groups through the Shire at twilight as the lanterns are lit — the hedgerows, hobbit holes, and The Green Dragon Inn glowing in fading light. The experience ends with a feast inside the Green Dragon featuring New Zealand produce and Southfarthing ales brewed exclusively for the venue. The setting on Alexander Farm in the Waikato's rolling hill country is itself beautiful at dusk. The evening format avoids day-tour crowds and allows the carefully crafted theatrical world to breathe.

reviews.summary: The evening banquet transforms an already excellent attraction into something genuinely magical. The lantern-lit setting at dusk is breathtaking. Worth every cent of the premium over the daytime tour.

pois.name: Timber Trail — Pureora Forest Park
pois.short: One of NZ's Great Rides — an 85km two-day mountain bike journey through ancient podocarp forest on a DOC trail.
pois.commentary: The Timber Trail is rated one of New Zealand's finest Great Rides — 85km through the heart of Pureora Forest Park, traversing historic logging country that inspired New Zealand's conservation movement. The trail runs from Poro-o-tarao in the north to Ōkahukura in the south over two days, crossing 14 suspension bridges (the longest spanning 141m), and passing through towering rimu, kahikatea, and tōtara forest. The forest is one of the last lowland podocarp forests in the North Island; kaka, kererū, and robins are heard and often seen. Shuttle services, hut and lodge accommodation, and bike hire are all available. Suitable for confident beginners on e-bikes.

reviews.summary: Universally praised as one of New Zealand's best cycling experiences. The suspension bridges are engineering spectacles. The forest is cathedral-like. Two of the best days you can spend on a bike in NZ.

pois.name: Forgotten World Highway (SH43) — Northern Entry at Taumarunui
pois.short: One of New Zealand's most remote and dramatic drives — SH43 from Taumarunui to Stratford through roadless King Country.
pois.commentary: The Forgotten World Highway (SH43) begins at Taumarunui in the Waikato and runs 150km south through remote hill country to Stratford in Taranaki. The road passes through a one-lane unlit rock tunnel (navigate by torchlight), the ghost town of Whangamomona (which declared itself an independent republic), and landscapes of staggering remoteness. Allow 4–5 hours with stops. Fuel and phone coverage are both absent for long stretches — carry extra fuel and let someone know your plans. One of the great North Island road experiences and almost entirely overlooked by passing traffic.

reviews.summary: A road trip bucket-list item. The scale of the emptiness is hard to convey — you genuinely feel off the grid. Whangamomona Republic Hotel is a mandatory stop.

pois.name: Waikato River Trail — Lake Karāpiro to Ātiamuri
pois.short: A 70km Great Ride from Lake Karāpiro to Lake Ātiamuri along the Waikato River — hydroelectric dams, native bush, and river views.
pois.commentary: The Waikato River Trail runs 70km along the eastern bank of the Waikato River through a series of hydroelectric lakes — Karāpiro, Whakamaru, Māraetai, Waipā, Ārapuni, and Ātiamuri — representing one of NZ's most significant engineering projects. The trail passes through native bush reserves and farmland, with long stretches of elevated river-edge riding. Suitable for confident intermediate cyclists; sections are challenging. Shuttle services allow one-way rides. The rowing complex at Lake Karāpiro — home to NZ's rowing Olympic training programme — is worth a visit at the start.

reviews.summary: A different perspective on the Waikato's hydroelectric landscape. The combination of engineering history and natural beauty is well-balanced. Well-suited to e-bikes for those wanting to cover the full distance.

pois.name: Hamilton to Raglan Coastal Drive (Whāingaroa)
pois.short: The drive from Hamilton to Raglan is one of the Waikato's finest — harbour views, black-sand beaches, and west coast drama.
pois.commentary: The 45-minute drive from Hamilton to Raglan on SH23 and beyond delivers a progressive reveal of the west coast. The Waikato plains give way to rolling hills, the Whāingaroa Harbour opens dramatically, and the road winds to Raglan township past pohutukawa and coastal farmland. Continuing south beyond Raglan, the road passes Whale Bay (Karioi rising steeply to your left), Ngarunui Beach (surf beach, lifeguards in summer), and ends at the Te Toto Gorge carpark. Return via the harbour back road for a different perspective on the estuary. Best timed for late afternoon when harbour light is extraordinary at low tide.

reviews.summary: One of the Waikato's most rewarding drives. The harbour light is exceptional. Build in time for a swim at Ngarunui and a meal in Raglan — one of the best food towns in the region.

pois.name: Hauraki Rail Trail — Thames to Paeroa
pois.short: Part of a 168km trail network across the Hauraki Plains — flat riding through wetlands, river gorges, and old goldfields country.
pois.commentary: The Hauraki Rail Trail is the largest rail trail network in New Zealand — 168km of connected trails running from Thames to Ōpōtiki via the Karangahake Gorge. The southern Waikato sections from Thames to Paeroa are the flattest and most accessible, crossing the Hauraki Plains through farmland and wetland channels. The trail passes through Te Aroha (mineral spa town, beautiful Victorian buildings) and Paeroa (home of Lemon and Paeroa soft drink), before entering the Karangahake Gorge where it traverses old gold-mining tunnels. The entire trail is suitable for families on e-bikes; sections are appropriate for children from about age 8.

reviews.summary: The Karangahake Gorge section is the undisputed highlight — riding through gold-mining tunnels is unforgettable. The flat Plains sections are ideal for families and e-bikes. Well-serviced and well-signposted.

pois.name: Cambridge Thoroughbred Country Scenic Drive
pois.short: The heartland of NZ's thoroughbred industry — a pastoral drive through stud farms and rolling Waikato countryside.
pois.commentary: The triangle between Cambridge, Te Awamutu, and Hamilton contains more thoroughbred horse studs per square kilometre than almost anywhere in the Southern Hemisphere. The country roads through Leamington and Hautapu pass paddock after paddock of broodmares and yearlings — studs including Waikato Stud (which bred champion Zabeel), Haunui Farm, and Cambridge Stud. New Zealand's mild winter climate and rich volcanic soils produce exceptional horses; the country punches far above its weight internationally in flat racing. Several studs welcome visitors by appointment. The drive is beautiful pastoral country regardless of horse interest.

reviews.summary: Beautiful pastoral countryside with the added interest of world-class horse breeding. Best in spring when foals are in the paddocks. The stud farm tours are fascinating if arranged in advance.

pois.name: Waitomo–Marokopa Falls–Coastal Loop
pois.short: A rewarding loop west of Waitomo through limestone country, natural bridges, and the remote Marokopa coast.
pois.commentary: The Marokopa Road west of Waitomo descends through increasingly wild limestone country to the Tasman coast. Along the way it passes Mangapōhue Natural Bridge (a 17m limestone arch — 5 minutes walk), Piripiri Caves (self-guided, free), and the 35m Marokopa Falls — one of the most photogenic in the Waikato. The road then drops to the black-sand coast at Marokopa, a remote fishing settlement. The return loop via Te Anga and the Awakino Gorge road adds limestone bluffs and the Mangaotaki Valley. A full day loop of approximately 180km from Waitomo, mostly on sealed roads — one of the most complete scenic drives in the Waikato.

reviews.summary: A spectacular and underrated road trip combining the best of Waikato's limestone country with wild west coast scenery. Marokopa Falls is outstanding. The full loop is highly recommended.

pois.name: Taupo Bungy and Cliffhanger Swing
pois.short: A dramatic bungee platform 47 metres over the Waikato River at Taupo, operated by AJ Hackett, with the additional option of a cliff-face pendulum swing.
pois.commentary: AJ Hackett's Taupo Bungy is located at Spa Road on the cliff edge above the Waikato River, 47 metres above the water, and is the only water-touch bungy in New Zealand's North Island. The platform cantilevers over the cliff face and offers jumpers a plunge towards the turquoise Waikato River with the option of dunking their head — or entire body — in the river at the bottom of the fall. The Cliffhanger Swing is a different adrenaline experience available at the same site, where up to three people can be harnessed together and released in a pendulum arc over the river gorge. The location above the Waikato River is dramatic and the Taupo volcanic plateau context adds geological interest to the experience.

reviews.summary: The Taupo Bungy earns consistently high ratings for the combination of dramatic gorge setting, water-touch option, and the quality of AJ Hackett's safety and guest experience operations. The Cliffhanger Swing is popular with groups.

pois.name: Huka Falls and Honey Hive Taupo
pois.short: New Zealand's most visited natural attraction — the Waikato River forced through a narrow rock channel producing an extraordinary blue-white torrent below Taupo.
pois.commentary: Huka Falls sits 2 kilometres north of Taupo town where the broad Waikato River is abruptly constricted to a 15-metre channel and drops 11 metres, forcing 220,000 litres of water per second through the narrow gorge in a thundering, brilliantly blue-white cascade. The colour results from oxygen forced into the water under pressure, creating an otherworldly intensity that makes the falls appear illuminated from within. Viewing platforms on both sides of the gorge provide close proximity to the falls, and jet boat tours depart from the Huka Falls boat landing to approach the base of the cascade from the downstream side. The falls are free to visit and the walking track from Taupo along the Waikato River is one of the finest short riverside walks in the North Island.

reviews.summary: The extraordinary colour and power of Huka Falls makes it genuinely impressive regardless of prior expectations, and the free access and short walk from the carpark make it one of New Zealand's best value natural highlights.

pois.name: Waitomo Black Water Rafting
pois.short: Float through glowworm-lit underground caves on an inner tube.
pois.commentary: Black water rafting through the Waitomo Caves is one of New Zealand's most iconic adventure activities. Participants don wetsuits and helmets, climb down into the Ruakuri Cave system, and float through pitch-dark underground passages on inflated inner tubes, illuminated only by the ethereal blue-green light of millions of glowworms above. The experience includes jumping off small waterfalls and squeezing through narrow passages. No prior experience is needed but reasonable fitness is required.

reviews.summary: An unforgettable underground adventure. Floating beneath glowworms in silence is magical. The guides are expert.

pois.name: Hobbiton Movie Set
pois.short: The original Lord of the Rings and Hobbit film set preserved as a permanent attraction.
pois.commentary: Hobbiton Movie Set near Matamata is the fully preserved filming location of the Shire from Peter Jackson's The Lord of the Rings and The Hobbit trilogies. The 12-acre set features 44 hobbit holes, the Green Dragon Inn, the Party Tree, and the mill, all maintained in immaculate condition among rolling Waikato farmland. Guided tours run every 20 minutes and last two hours, ending with a complimentary drink at the Green Dragon. Booking ahead is essential.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary creation even for non-fans. The attention to detail is breathtaking. The Green Dragon drink is a perfect finale.

pois.name: Turangawaewae Marae / Ngaruawahia
pois.short: Seat of the Maori King movement and most significant marae in Waikato.
pois.commentary: Turangawaewae Marae in Ngaruawahia is the principal marae of the Kingitanga (Maori King movement) and the official residence of the reigning Maori monarch. The annual Ngaruawahia Regatta in March opens the marae grounds to the public for waka races, cultural performances, and kapa haka. Outside of public events, the marae is not generally open to tourists and visitors should respect its significance as a living cultural institution.

reviews.summary: A deeply significant cultural site. The regatta is a wonderful public occasion. Respect the marae's private nature at other times.

pois.name: Bridal Veil Falls / Waireinga
pois.short: Stunning 55m waterfall cascading into a bush-fringed pool near Raglan.
pois.commentary: Bridal Veil Falls (Waireinga) is a spectacular 55-metre plunge waterfall near Raglan, accessible via a short bush walk from the car park. The upper viewpoint offers a dramatic view of the falls from above, while the lower track descends 258 steps to the base pool where the full height of the cascade is revealed. The falls plunge into a rock amphitheatre surrounded by lush native bush. Swimming in the pool is possible but the water is cold. The surrounding Pakoka River valley is rich in native bush and birdlife. The falls are a 20-minute drive from Raglan, making them easy to combine with a surf trip. The track is well-maintained and suitable for moderate fitness levels.

reviews.summary: A stunning waterfall that's easy to access. The lower viewpoint is more impressive. Worth the 258 steps down (and back up).

pois.name: Mangapohue Natural Bridge
pois.short: Massive limestone arch with oyster fossils in ancient canyon near Waitomo.
pois.commentary: The Mangapohue Natural Bridge is a massive 17-metre-high limestone arch — the remnant of an ancient cave system — spanning a narrow canyon near Waitomo. The short walk (20 minutes return) through the canyon passes towering limestone walls embedded with 35-million-year-old oyster fossils, the remains of a tropical seabed long since uplifted. The natural bridge itself is an imposing formation that frames a view of native bush and sky above. The canyon floor features ferns, mosses, and twisted tree roots clinging to the rock walls. After dark, glowworms illuminate the canyon walls, creating a free alternative to commercial cave tours. The site is a short drive from Waitomo village and makes an excellent complement to the cave visits.

reviews.summary: A quick, easy walk to an impressive natural formation. The oyster fossils are fascinating. Free glowworms at night are a bonus.

pois.name: Firth Tower Museum / Matamata
pois.short: 1882 concrete tower and heritage village showcasing Waikato rural history.
pois.commentary: Firth Tower is a striking four-storey concrete tower built in 1882 by Auckland businessman Josiah Clifton Firth as a granary and lookout on his model farm. The tower anchors a museum complex of relocated heritage buildings including a homestead, schoolhouse, church, blacksmith shop, and jail that tell the story of Waikato rural life in the colonial era. The museum is one of the most comprehensive regional heritage collections in the North Island, with detailed displays on early farming, Maori-European relations, and the development of the dairy industry. The surrounding grounds are pleasant for a stroll. The museum is run by passionate volunteers. Matamata itself is best known as the gateway to Hobbiton, but this museum offers a real rather than fictional history.

reviews.summary: Surprisingly comprehensive rural history museum. The heritage buildings are charming. Volunteers add warmth and knowledge.

pois.name: Te Aroha Mineral Spas
pois.short: Historic mineral hot springs at the base of Mount Te Aroha with heritage bath house.
pois.commentary: Te Aroha sits at the base of its namesake mountain and is one of the few places in the world where naturally hot soda water springs bubble to the surface. The Te Aroha Mineral Spas operate from a beautifully restored Edwardian bath house, offering private mineral hot pools in heritage surroundings. The soda springs in the adjacent domain are one of only two such hot soda springs known worldwide. The town's heritage precinct retains Victorian and Edwardian commercial buildings from its spa town heyday. The walk to the summit of Mount Te Aroha (952m) takes 3-4 hours return and rewards with panoramic views over the Hauraki Plains and Waikato. The town makes a relaxing base for exploring the eastern Waikato.

reviews.summary: Unique mineral springs in a heritage setting. The private pools are relaxing. The mountain walk adds an active dimension.

pois.name: Ruakuri Cave
pois.short: Longest underground walking tour in NZ with glowworms, waterfalls, and limestone formations.
pois.commentary: Ruakuri Cave offers the longest underground walking tour in New Zealand, descending through a dramatic spiral entrance into a vast limestone cave system featuring glowworm displays, underground waterfalls, and intricate stalactite and stalagmite formations. The 1.6-kilometre walkway takes about 90 minutes and is fully wheelchair and pushchair accessible — a remarkable engineering achievement underground. The cave's name means den of dogs, referring to the packs of wild dogs that once inhabited the entrance. The experience is more immersive and less crowded than the neighbouring Waitomo Glowworm Caves, making it many visitors' preferred option. The cave was sacred to local Maori and was closed for decades before being reopened with a new entrance that bypasses the culturally sensitive original cave mouth.

reviews.summary: Many say this is better than the more famous Glowworm Cave. Longer, more varied, and less crowded. Wheelchair accessible.

pois.name: Cambridge / Town of Trees
pois.short: Elegant heritage town known for thoroughbred horse studs and tree-lined streets.
pois.commentary: Cambridge is one of New Zealand's most attractive small towns, earning its nickname Town of Trees from the majestic English oaks, planes, and elms that line its streets and parks. The town is the centre of New Zealand's thoroughbred horse breeding industry, with world-class studs in the surrounding rolling countryside. Cambridge Stud, home of champion sire Doyen, offers occasional open days. The main street features excellent antique shops, boutiques, and cafes in well-preserved heritage buildings. The Cambridge Museum covers local Maori and European history. Lake Karapiro nearby hosted the 2010 World Rowing Championships. The town's English village atmosphere, combined with excellent cafes and shops, makes it a pleasant stop on the road between Auckland and Rotorua.

reviews.summary: Genuinely charming small town with beautiful tree-lined streets. Great cafes and antique shopping. Horse country adds character.

pois.name: Waitomo Glowworm Caves
pois.short: World-famous underground boat ride beneath a galaxy of living glowworms.
pois.commentary: The Waitomo Glowworm Caves are one of New Zealand's most iconic natural attractions — an underground limestone cave system where a boat ride glides silently beneath a galaxy of thousands of native glowworms (Arachnocampa luminosa). The blue-green bioluminescence of the glowworm larvae, hanging from silk threads on the cave ceiling, creates a mesmerising display that resembles a starlit sky. The cave system also features impressive limestone formations — stalactites, stalagmites, and the Cathedral chamber with its remarkable acoustics. Three separate cave experiences are available at Waitomo: the classic Glowworm Cave, Ruakuri Cave (wheelchair accessible), and Aranui Cave. Black water rafting through the caves offers an adventure alternative.

reviews.summary: A genuinely magical underground experience. The glowworm boat ride is unforgettable. Multiple cave options for different interests.

pois.name: Raglan — Surf Town & Bridal Veil Falls
pois.short: NZ's legendary surf town with world-class left-hand break, excellent cafes, and a stunning waterfall nearby.
pois.commentary: Raglan is New Zealand's most famous surf town, home to Manu Bay — one of the longest and most consistent left-hand point breaks in the world. The town itself has a relaxed, bohemian character with excellent cafes, a strong arts community, and a laid-back vibe that's attracted surfers and creative types for decades. Surf lessons are available for beginners at Ngarunui Beach. Nearby Bridal Veil Falls (Waireinga) is one of the Waikato's most spectacular waterfalls — a 55-metre free-falling cascade accessible via a short bush walk. The combination of world-class surfing, creative culture, and natural beauty makes Raglan one of the Waikato's essential destinations.

reviews.summary: Legendary surf town with genuine character. The left-hand break is world famous. Bridal Veil Falls is spectacular. Great cafes.

pois.name: Matamata Hobbit-Themed i-SITE
pois.short: Hobbit-themed visitor centre — gateway to Hobbiton with themed information centre.
pois.commentary: Matamata's i-SITE visitor centre has fully embraced its connection to Hobbiton with a themed exterior and interior that channels Middle-earth. While primarily a booking centre for Hobbiton tours, it's worth a visit in its own right for the photo opportunities and film memorabilia. The town of Matamata has adopted its Hobbit connection enthusiastically, with Hobbit-themed signage and references throughout. The i-SITE staff can arrange Hobbiton tickets and provide information on the wider Waikato region. Even if you're not doing a Hobbiton tour, the centre captures the creativity of the New Zealand film industry's impact on regional tourism.

reviews.summary: Fun Hobbit-themed centre, mainly useful as a booking/info stop. Good photo opportunities. Gateway to the real Hobbiton nearby.

pois.name: Te Awamutu Rose Gardens & Museum
pois.short: Award-winning rose gardens and a museum in the hometown of Split Enz and Crowded House's Finn brothers.
pois.commentary: Te Awamutu is known as the Rose Town of New Zealand, with its award-winning municipal rose gardens containing over 2,000 bushes spanning heritage and modern varieties. The gardens are at their peak from November to April. The Te Awamutu Museum covers local Waikato history, including the significant Battle of Ōrākau (1864) during the New Zealand Wars. The town is also famous as the hometown of Neil and Tim Finn (of Split Enz and Crowded House fame), and the museum has a permanent exhibition on the brothers' musical career. The combination of roses, military history, and musical heritage makes Te Awamutu a surprisingly diverse stop.

reviews.summary: Beautiful rose gardens and an interesting museum. The Finn brothers exhibition is fun for music fans. Worth a stop.

pois.name: Raglan Surf Breaks / Whāingaroa
pois.short: NZ's most famous surf town with legendary left-hand point breaks.
pois.commentary: Raglan (Whāingaroa) is New Zealand's most celebrated surf destination, home to a series of world-class left-hand point breaks that are among the longest and most consistent in the world. Manu Bay and Whale Bay offer powerful, peeling waves that draw surfers from around the globe, while the nearby Ngarunui Beach (also called Ocean Beach) provides more forgiving conditions for learners with several surf schools operating. The town itself has evolved into a creative, alternative community with excellent cafés, boutique shops, and a thriving arts scene. The harbour area is beautiful for paddleboarding and kayaking. Raglan was named NZ's best surf beach in 2025. The town is about 45 minutes from Hamilton and makes an excellent day trip or weekend escape.

reviews.summary: Surfers rate the waves as world-class. The town has great character and excellent cafés. Surf lessons are available for beginners. A must-visit for anyone who loves the ocean.

pois.name: Waiwhakareke Natural Heritage Park
pois.short: Ecological restoration project recreating pre-human native ecosystems in Hamilton.
pois.commentary: Waiwhakareke is an ambitious project to restore 60 hectares of Hamilton's urban fringe to its pre-human state, with wetlands, bush, and rolling landscapes being replanted. Walking tracks wind through the emerging ecosystem, offering a unique view of ecological restoration in action.

reviews.summary: Fascinating restoration project — worth visiting to see the progress.

pois.name: Classics Museum — Hamilton
pois.short: Immaculate collection of classic cars, hot rods, and Americana memorabilia.
pois.commentary: The Classics Museum in Hamilton houses over 100 immaculately restored vehicles from the 1920s to 1970s, set in themed displays with period memorabilia. From gleaming Cadillacs to Kiwi-built hot rods, the collection appeals well beyond car enthusiasts.

reviews.summary: Stunning collection — even non-car people love it.

pois.name: Raglan Surf School & Ngarunui Beach
pois.short: Learn to surf at one of the world's longest left-hand breaks.
pois.commentary: Raglan is home to one of the world's most consistent left-hand point breaks, and the beginner-friendly Ngarunui Beach is perfect for lessons. Local surf schools offer personalised instruction in a laid-back atmosphere that captures Raglan's surf culture perfectly.

reviews.summary: Perfect surf town — the instructors and waves are brilliant.

pois.name: Firth Tower Museum — Matamata
pois.short: Historic 1882 concrete tower and colonial village museum in Matamata.
pois.commentary: Firth Tower is a unique 1882 concrete tower built by Auckland industrialist Josiah Clifton Firth. The surrounding museum village includes relocated colonial buildings — a school, church, jail, and homestead — telling the story of Waikato pioneer settlement.

reviews.summary: Interesting heritage village — the tower itself is unique.

pois.name: Kawhia Hot Water Beach — Ocean Beach
pois.short: Dig your own hot pool on this wild west coast beach — the locals' Hot Water Beach.
pois.commentary: Kawhia's Ocean Beach offers the same dig-your-own-hot-pool experience as the famous Coromandel Hot Water Beach, but without the crowds. At low tide, hot water bubbles up through the black sand on this wild west coast beach — a genuine hidden gem known mainly to locals.

reviews.summary: Like Hot Water Beach without the tourists — a real hidden gem.

pois.name: Waikato River Trails — Lake Karāpiro
pois.short: Multi-day cycling trail along the Waikato River connecting hydro lakes.
pois.commentary: The Waikato River Trails offer over 100 kilometres of cycling through bush, farmland, and past a series of hydro dams on the Waikato River. The Lake Karāpiro section passes the world-class rowing venue, suspension bridges, and bush-clad gorges — a multi-day cycling adventure.

reviews.summary: Excellent trail system — the suspension bridges are exciting.

pois.name: Lake Ngāroto
pois.short: Waipā's largest peat lake, with a loop track, restoration planting, birdlife and deep local history.
pois.commentary: Lake Ngāroto sits close to Te Awamutu but feels like a different Waikato rhythm: open water, wetland edges, restoration planting and a mostly flat loop track. Waipā District Council identifies it as the largest of the district's peat lakes and records significant history around the lake. The modern visit is part recreation, part ecological repair. Walk the loop, watch for birds and notice the restoration work rather than treating the lake as just another picnic stop. Because the place carries deep cultural and historical associations, this story should keep being improved with mana whenua guidance.
pois.audio_story: Lake Ngāroto sits close to Te Awamutu but feels like a different Waikato rhythm: open water, wetland edges, restoration planting and a mostly flat loop track. Waipā District Council identifies it as the largest of the district's peat lakes and records significant history around the lake. The modern visit is part recreation, part ecological repair. Walk the loop, watch for birds and notice the restoration work rather than treating the lake as just another picnic stop. Because the place carries deep cultural and historical associations, this story should keep being improved with mana whenua guidance.

reviews.summary: A popular easy loop for locals, birdwatchers and families; visitors value the open lake views and visible restoration work.

pois.name: Taitua Arboretum
pois.short: A peaceful Hamilton arboretum with lakes, trees, birds and easy trails west of the city.
pois.commentary: Taitua Arboretum is Hamilton’s slow garden escape. Paths pass ponds, mature trees and birdlife, making it ideal when you need a green hour that is neither formal garden nor backcountry walk.
pois.audio_story: Taitua Arboretum is Hamilton’s slow garden escape. Paths pass ponds, mature trees and birdlife, making it ideal when you need a green hour that is neither formal garden nor backcountry walk.

pois.name: Nikau Cave and Café
pois.short: A guided limestone cave and rural café experience on Waikato’s west-coast backroads.
pois.commentary: Nikau Cave feels like the antidote to overproduced cave tourism. The guided trip is personal and muddy enough to feel real, with glowworms, stream passages and a café stop that makes the rural detour feel complete.
pois.audio_story: Nikau Cave feels like the antidote to overproduced cave tourism. The guided trip is personal and muddy enough to feel real, with glowworms, stream passages and a café stop that makes the rural detour feel complete.

pois.name: Lake Rotopiko / National Wetland Centre
pois.short: A restored peat-lake wetland complex south of Hamilton, focused on native fish, birds and education.
pois.commentary: Lake Rotopiko shows the Waikato below the pasture line: peat lakes, reeds, native fish and wetland restoration. It is a quiet but important stop for understanding what this region has lost and what patient repair can bring back.

pois.name: Raglan Wharf
pois.short: A working-harbour wharf precinct in Raglan with fish, coffee, galleries and sunset water views.
pois.commentary: Raglan Wharf is where the town’s surf-town ease meets working harbour practicality. Fish shops, artists, coffee and boat activity share the same wooden-edge atmosphere, making it a good first stop before the beaches claim the day.

pois.name: Te Awa River Ride
pois.short: A riverside cycle trail following the Waikato River through Hamilton, Cambridge and linked communities.
pois.commentary: Te Awa River Ride puts the Waikato River back at the centre of movement. The trail connects towns, bridges, parks and river bends, giving cyclists a way to feel the region’s spine rather than just drive parallel to it.

pois.name: Hakarimata Summit Track
pois.short: A famously stair-heavy climb above Ngāruawāhia to views over the Waikato basin.
pois.commentary: Hakarimata Summit Track is simple in concept and very honest in execution: stairs, sweat, forest, lookout. Locals use it for fitness, visitors use it for perspective, and everyone earns the same wide Waikato view from the top.

pois.name: Karamu Walkway
pois.short: A farm walkway west of Hamilton with rolling hill views toward Pirongia and the wider Waikato.
pois.commentary: Karamu Walkway is Waikato hill country in walking form. The route crosses farmland and ridges, offering the kind of broad, pastoral views that explain the region just as clearly as its famous caves and gardens.

pois.name: Okoroire Hot Springs
pois.short: Historic private hot pools in a wooded gully beside the old Okoroire hotel.
pois.commentary: Okoroire Hot Springs feels like a pocket of older Waikato travel. The thermal pools sit down a path in a bushy gully, paired with a heritage hotel atmosphere that makes the stop feel more like a detour from another era than a modern spa product.

pois.short: Pirongia’s heritage centre helps explain a small town with outsized frontier history, from military redoubt stories to mountain-edge settlement.

pois.name: Te Toto Gorge Lookout
pois.short: A dramatic roadside lookout near Raglan, facing layered cliffs, surf coast and old garden terraces.
pois.commentary: Te Toto Gorge is one of Raglan’s sharpest pauses: a short stop with a long view. From the lookout the coast drops away in cliffs and folds, the Tasman works below, and the shape of the land makes it clear why this western edge has always pulled people toward it.

pois.name: Kaniwhaniwha Caves
pois.short: A Pirongia Forest Park walk to small limestone caves and cool stream-side bush.
pois.commentary: The Kaniwhaniwha Caves make Pirongia feel playful as well as grand. The track follows forest and stream country before narrowing into limestone pockets, where the reward is less about scale than the fun of discovering a hidden shape in the mountain’s lower folds.
